serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of approximately 250 g, comprising smoked duck ham slices over a bed of organic mesclun greens, garnished with toasted sesame seeds, sliced red chili, and drizzled with balsamic sherry vinaigrette.

nutrient_source.title: Duck, domesticated, meat only, cooked, roasted — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 05143); supplemented with USDA entries for mesclun greens, sesame seeds, balsamic vinegar, and olive oil for vinaigrette component
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for smoked duck ham salad exists in any approved primary source. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis: approximately 40% smoked/cooked duck meat (USDA SR Legacy NDB 05143), 35% mesclun greens (USDA FDC 169248), 10% toasted sesame seeds (USDA FDC 169407), 5% red chili (USDA FDC 170108), and 10% balsamic sherry vinaigrette (estimated from balsamic vinegar USDA FDC 173469 and olive oil USDA FDC 171413). Smoking and curing of duck ham increases sodium content; an upward adjustment of approximately 30% on sodium has been applied to reflect curing. Values represent a per-100g composite estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; mixed salad with protein and fat components analogous estimate; mesclun greens GI ~15, vinaigrette dressing negligible, duck protein-dominant component suppresses overall GI
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for smoked duck ham salad. GI estimated at 32 based on analogous mixed protein-fat-vegetable salad entries from Atkinson et al. 2021 (AJCN). Mesclun greens have a very low GI (~15); sesame seeds and duck protein further suppress glycemic response. Balsamic vinegar has been shown to reduce postprandial glycemia (Brighenti et al., Eur J Clin Nutr 1995). Composite GI of 32 is a conservative low estimate. GL per serving = (32 × 5.2 × 2.5) / 100 = 4.2.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from smoked and cured duck ham
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be cautious with this dish as the smoked duck ham contributes approximately 520 mg sodium per 100 g (estimated ~1300 mg per serving), which represents over 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; portion control or requesting reduced-sodium preparation is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index driven by protein, fat, and vinegar components
type2_diabetes.guidance: This salad has an estimated glycemic index of 32 (low), making it a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the high protein content from duck ham, healthy fats from sesame seeds and vinaigrette, and the acetic acid in balsamic sherry vinegar collectively blunt postprandial blood glucose response, though total carbohydrate intake should still be monitored within the individual's daily plan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat and elevated sodium from cured duck ham
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While the dish contains beneficial monounsaturated fats from the vinaigrette and sesame seeds, the cured duck ham contributes moderate saturated fat (~2.6 g/100 g) and high sodium (~520 mg/100 g), both of which are risk factors for cardiovascular disease; individuals with CVD should limit portion size and prefer this dish as an occasional rather than regular menu choice.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Anatra (GF) is an Italian-inspired smoked duck ham salad served at Ricciotti restaurant in Singapore. The dish features inhouse-prepared smoked duck ham — cured and cold-smoked duck breast sliced thinly — arranged over a bed of organic mesclun greens, a mix of young tender salad leaves including arugula, frisée, radicchio, and mâche. The salad is garnished with toasted sesame seeds for nutty crunch, sliced red chili for mild heat, and dressed with a balsamic sherry vinaigrette — a tangy-sweet emulsion of aged balsamic vinegar, sherry vinegar, and olive oil. The dish is gluten-free (GF designation) and dairy-free. Nutritionally, it is a protein-moderate, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate salad with an estimated 165 kcal per 100 g. The duck ham provides complete animal protein (~12.5 g/100 g) and iron, while sesame seeds contribute calcium, zinc, and vitamin E. The mesclun greens supply vitamin A, vitamin C, and folate. The balsamic sherry vinaigrette adds polyphenols and acetic acid, which may attenuate postprandial glycemia. The estimated glycemic index is 32 (low), making it appropriate for blood glucose management. Sodium content is elevated due to the curing process (~520 mg/100 g), which warrants caution for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. This dish is suitable as a light main course or starter in an Italian restaurant context and is well-suited for low-carbohydrate, gluten-free, and high-protein dietary patterns.
common_user_queries: Is the duck salad at Ricciotti gluten free?
Calories in smoked duck ham salad
Is smoked duck salad good for diabetics?
Duck salad with balsamic vinaigrette calories

confidence_reason: No exact entry for smoked duck ham salad exists in any approved primary nutritional database. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked duck meat, mesclun greens, sesame seeds, red chili, balsamic vinegar, and olive oil. GI is an analogous estimate based on Atkinson et al. 2021 (AJCN). Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are individually well-supported in USDA FoodData Central, and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from 'Grains and staples' (input) to 'Meat and alternatives' as the dish is protein-dominant (smoked duck ham). 2. Cuisine tags 'Pasta', 'Pizza', 'Chicken Dish' from field survey input are not applicable to this dish and have been discarded; the dish is a duck salad. 3. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reliably reported in the USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this estimate; duck meat contains trace vitamin D but the composite salad value is not determinable from available data. 4. Trans fat is reported as 0.1 g/100 g reflecting naturally occurring trans fats (vaccenic acid) in duck meat at trace levels; this is not industrially produced trans fat. 5. Sodium is elevated due to the curing and smoking process applied to the duck ham; an upward adjustment of approximately 30% was applied to the base USDA cooked duck sodium value to reflect curing. 6. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. 7. Halal status is not confirmed as the restaurant (Ricciotti) is not listed as a halal-certified establishment in Singapore; is_halal set to false. 8. The dish name 'Anatra' is Italian for 'duck'; the GF suffix denotes gluten-free. 9. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA component sources and are not individually confirmed by a single approved source entry. 10. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component sources used for this composite estimate.
